docs.title: repo-guide
docs.text:
The repo-guide repository is a Python project that uses AI to generate documentation for code repositories. It leverages large language models (LLMs) to understand the structure and content of a repository and create Markdown guides. The generated documentation is then served using MkDocs.

The project has the following dependencies:

  • bleach and bleach-allowlist: Used to sanitize HTML in the generated documentation.
  • click: Used to create a command-line interface for the repo-guide application.
  • gitpython: Used to interact with Git repositories to understand file history and identify changes.
  • llm and llm-gemini: Used to interface with LLMs, specifically Google's Gemini models.
  • mkdocs and mkdocs-material: Used to generate and serve the documentation website.
  • tqdm: Used to display progress bars during documentation generation.
  • tiktoken: Used to count tokens for language model input and output.
  • magika: (optional dependency) Used to classify file types and ignore binary files.

The repository is structured as follows:

  • The tests directory contains a comprehensive suite of tests for the repo-guide application. It includes tests for prompt construction, handling of various file types, the construction of repository and file URLs, and end-to-end documentation generation. It uses fixtures to mock dependencies like the LLM and the repository itself.
  • The src directory contains the source code for the repo-guide application.
    • The src/repo_guide subdirectory contains the core logic of the application, including the command-line interface defined in cli.py. This file is responsible for parsing command-line arguments, creating a DocGenerator, and setting up the documentation server. The DocGenerator class handles loading existing documentation, filtering files, building prompts for the LLM, generating documentation, managing tokens, and writing the mkdocs configuration. The files __init__.py and __main__.py make the repo_guide directory a Python package and provide a way to invoke the CLI from the command line.
  • The _github directory contains configuration files for GitHub features.
    • The _github/workflows directory contains the GitHub Actions workflows for testing, publishing the package to PyPI, and publishing the documentation website. The workflows defined in test.yml, publish.yml, and publish_site.yml handle continuous integration, deployment to PyPI, and deployment of the documentation website to GitHub Pages.
  • The file pyproject.toml defines project metadata, dependencies, and build system settings. It also configures the ruff linter.
  • The file README.md provides an overview of the repo-guide project, installation instructions, usage examples, troubleshooting tips, and development guidelines.
  • The file LICENSE contains the Apache 2.0 license under which the project is distributed.
  • The file pre-commit-config.yaml configures the pre-commit hooks to enforce code quality using ruff.
  • The file gitignore specifies files and directories that should be ignored by Git.
  • The file .python-version specifies the Python version used by the project.
  • The file .gitattributes specifies how Git should handle line endings.

The core logic of repo-guide is encapsulated in the DocGenerator class, which is instantiated and invoked by the command-line interface in cli.py. The DocGenerator class reads file contents and structure from a Git repository and uses a custom XML format to generate prompts for the LLM, which produces the documentation included in the generated README.md files. The mkdocs configuration generated by repo-guide uses the bleach library to sanitize HTML in the documentation.

docs.title: .github
docs.text:
The repo-guide/.github directory contains configuration files for GitHub features, such as workflows.

The workflows subdirectory contains files defining the continuous integration and continuous deployment (CI/CD) workflows for the repo-guide project using GitHub Actions.

  • test.yml defines a workflow that runs tests on every push and pull request. It uses a matrix strategy to test with Python 3.11 and 3.12. The workflow checks out the code, sets up Python with uv, installs project dependencies, and runs the tests using pytest.
  • publish.yml defines a workflow that publishes the Python package to PyPI when a release is created. The workflow includes two jobs: test and deploy. The test job is similar to the workflow defined in test.yml. The deploy job runs after successful tests and is responsible for building and publishing the package to PyPI, using an OpenID Connect token.
  • publish_site.yml defines a workflow that publishes the documentation site generated by repo-guide when a release is created or the workflow is manually dispatched. The workflow includes test and deploy jobs. The test job is similar to the workflow defined in test.yml. The deploy job checks out the code, configures git credentials, installs uv, sets up Python, caches dependencies for faster builds, builds the documentation using repo-guide, and deploys the documentation to GitHub Pages using mkdocs gh-deploy. The LLM_GEMINI_KEY is passed as an environment variable to repo-guide so that it can use Google's Gemini model to generate docs.

docs.title: workflows
docs.text:
The files in the repo-guide/.github/workflows directory define the continuous integration and continuous deployment (CI/CD) workflows for the repo-guide project using GitHub Actions.

  • test.yml defines a workflow that runs tests on every push and pull request. It uses a matrix strategy to test with Python 3.11 and 3.12. The workflow checks out the code, sets up Python with uv, installs project dependencies, and runs the tests using pytest.
  • publish.yml defines a workflow that publishes the Python package to PyPI when a release is created. The workflow includes two jobs: test and deploy. The test job is similar to the workflow defined in test.yml. The deploy job runs after successful tests and is responsible for building and publishing the package to PyPI, using an OpenID Connect token.
  • publish_site.yml defines a workflow that publishes the documentation site generated by repo-guide when a release is created or the workflow is manually dispatched. The workflow includes test and deploy jobs. The test job is similar to the workflow defined in test.yml. The deploy job checks out the code, configures git credentials, installs uv, sets up Python, caches dependencies for faster builds, builds the documentation using repo-guide, and deploys the documentation to GitHub Pages using mkdocs gh-deploy. The LLM_GEMINI_KEY is passed as an environment variable to repo-guide so that it can use Google's Gemini model to generate docs.

docs.title: src
docs.text:
The repo-guide/src directory serves as the source code root for the repo-guide application. It directly contains the core logic within the repo_guide subdirectory.

The repo_guide subdirectory contains the main code for the repo-guide application:

  • cli.py defines the command-line interface and orchestrates the documentation generation process. It handles command-line arguments, creates a DocGenerator, configures mkdocs, and starts the documentation server.
  • __init__.py is an empty file that indicates the repo_guide directory is a Python package, allowing its modules to be imported.
  • __main__.py makes the command-line interface defined in cli.py available when the package is run as a script (e.g. python -m repo_guide).

docs.title: repo_guide
docs.text:
The repo-guide/src/repo_guide directory contains the core logic for the repo-guide application.

The most important file is cli.py. This file defines the command-line interface for the application using the click library. It includes the main logic for:

  • Parsing command-line arguments such as the input directory, output directory, model to use, and various flags.
  • Creating a DocGenerator class that is responsible for generating the documentation.
  • Setting up the mkdocs configuration for the generated documentation and starting a server.

The DocGenerator class has several key responsibilities:

  • Loading Existing Docs: When resuming a previous documentation run, load_existing_docs finds existing README.md files in the output directory and stores them in memory.
  • Filtering Files: It uses git or magika to filter out binary files. It also uses file name patterns passed to the --ignore option.
  • Building Prompts: _build_prompt constructs a prompt for the LLM, which includes the directory structure and file contents using a custom XML format. The prompt includes any existing README.md files found in subdirectories.
  • Generating Documentation: The generate_docs method walks through the input directory, builds prompts, sends them to the LLM using exponential backoff (via _prompt_with_backoff), and writes the responses to README.md files in the output directory.
  • Token Management: The DocGenerator keeps track of tokens used and can be limited by the --token-budget and --files-token-limit options. The token limit for file contents prevents excessive prompt lengths.
  • Configuring mkdocs: The write_mkdocs_configuration function generates the mkdocs.yml and my_hooks.py files to configure the documentation site and sanitize HTML.

The __init__.py file is empty, indicating that this directory is a Python package.

The __main__.py file imports and calls the cli function from cli.py, making the command line interface available.

docs.title: tests
docs.text:
The tests directory contains the tests for the repo-guide project. It contains a single file, test_repo_guide.py, which contains a comprehensive suite of tests for various functionalities of the repo-guide application.

The most important tests are those that test the DocGenerator class and its methods. The tests cover:

  • Prompt construction using _build_prompt. This is tested with both the root directory of a test repository as well as a subdirectory. The prompt construction test verifies that the generated prompts for both directories contain the correct directory structure, file paths, file contents, and links to the file in the remote repository. The test uses dummy generated readmes that are included in the prompt as if they were written by the LLM. The generated prompts are then verified against expected outputs using string comparison.
  • Handling of files with different encodings, including those that cannot be decoded using common text encodings. The test verifies that these files are skipped.
  • The construction of the repository URL and file URL prefixes. It tests that the correct URLs are generated when the input directory is the root of the repository as well as when it is a subdirectory.
  • End-to-end documentation generation, using the CLI. The test verifies that the generated documentation exists and the generated content is correct. There are two end-to-end tests: one that tests the full repository and another that tests a subdirectory.

The tests make use of fixtures such as test_repo, which creates a test repository with a nested directory structure, a README, and git history, and mock_model, which mocks an LLM model that returns a truncated version of its input. These fixtures allow tests to be run without relying on external resources or an actual LLM.

These tests ensure the repo-guide application functions as intended and can generate documentation based on the content of a code repository.
